title: URL Shortener
description: Design a scalable URL shortening service that creates short, unique links and redirects users to original URLs

coreRequirements.label: URL Shortening
coreRequirements.description: Accept a long URL and return a short, compact alias.
examplePhrases: Submit a long URL to receive a short link.
System generates a compact alias for any valid URL.

coreRequirements.label: URL Redirection
coreRequirements.description: Resolve a short URL and redirect the client to the original long URL.
examplePhrases: Opening the short URL takes the user to the destination.
Service performs an HTTP 301 or 302 to the long URL.

coreRequirements.label: Uniqueness Guarantee
coreRequirements.description: Each short URL is unique and maps to exactly one long URL.
examplePhrases: Every short link is unique.
Use hashing, counters, or base62 to avoid collisions.

optionalRequirements.label: Custom Aliases
optionalRequirements.description: Allow users to provide a custom short code instead of a generated one.
examplePhrases: Users can set example.com/my-link.
Support vanity slugs chosen by users.

optionalRequirements.label: Link Expiration
optionalRequirements.description: Support optional expiration dates or TTL after which links are invalid.
examplePhrases: Links can expire after a set period.
Provide TTL for temporary URLs.

optionalRequirements.label: Analytics and Metrics
optionalRequirements.description: Track usage per short URL such as click counts and referrers.
examplePhrases: Track number of clicks per short URL.
Record referrers and basic usage stats.

optionalRequirements.label: User Authentication
optionalRequirements.description: Support accounts and API keys to manage owned links.
examplePhrases: Users can sign in to manage their URLs.
Programmatic access via API key.

optionalRequirements.label: URL Management and Deletion
optionalRequirements.description: List, edit, and delete created short URLs.
examplePhrases: Users can delete links they no longer need.
Dashboard to view and manage links.

coreRequirements.label: Performance and Latency
coreRequirements.description: Redirects should be fast. Aim for sub 100 ms p95 on the redirect path.
examplePhrases: Redirects should target p95 under 100 ms.
Keep overall responsiveness high for good UX.

coreRequirements.label: Scalability and Throughput
coreRequirements.description: Handle large read traffic with growth and spikes. Reference targets from the brief.
examplePhrases: Support about 1B reads per day which is about 10k rps on average.
Expect 10x to 100x spikes with writes in the millions per day and 1 to 5B lifetime URLs.

coreRequirements.label: Availability and Reliability
coreRequirements.description: Service should be highly available with no single points of failure.
examplePhrases: Target 99.9 percent availability.
Avoid single points of failure on hot paths.

optionalRequirements.label: Data Durability and Persistence
optionalRequirements.description: URL mappings should survive crashes. Use persistence, replication, and backups.
examplePhrases: Replicate the database and run regular backups.
Persist mappings to disk to prevent data loss.

optionalRequirements.label: Consistency Tradeoffs
optionalRequirements.description: Pick acceptable consistency per path. Redirects need strong mapping. Analytics can be eventual.
examplePhrases: Eventual consistency is fine for analytics counters.
Redirect lookups should return the latest mapping.

optionalRequirements.label: Security and Abuse Prevention
optionalRequirements.description: Mitigate abuse and attacks. Validate targets and control traffic.
examplePhrases: Apply rate limits and bot protection.
Block malicious or unsafe destination URLs.

optionalRequirements.label: Observability and Monitoring
optionalRequirements.description: Collect metrics, logs, traces, and set alerts for key SLOs.
examplePhrases: Alert on latency, error rate, and cache hit ratio.
Use tracing to debug cross service requests.

optionalRequirements.label: Caching Policy
optionalRequirements.description: Choose 302 to count every hit or 301 to favor client caching.
examplePhrases: Use 302 if analytics must count every request.
Use 301 for better client performance when analytics can be approximate.

optionalRequirements.label: Throughput and Latency Optimizations
optionalRequirements.description: Use caches for hot keys, DB indexes on short_url, and optional read replicas.
examplePhrases: Cache frequent short links in Redis.
Index short_url and add read replicas if needed.

optionalRequirements.label: ID Generation Resilience
optionalRequirements.description: If using a counter service, avoid a single point of failure and plan for contention.
examplePhrases: Shard counter ranges to remove a single bottleneck.
Fallback to random or hash based IDs on counter errors.

coreRequirements.label: URL Creation Endpoint
coreRequirements.description: POST endpoint to create shortened URLs with documented request/response

coreRequirements.label: Redirect Endpoint
coreRequirements.description: GET endpoint to redirect short URLs with documented behavior
examplePhrases:
GET /:code
Response: HTTP 302 redirect to original URL
Errors: 404 (not found), 410 (expired)
Behavior: Looks up code in database, returns redirect to stored long URL

optionalRequirements.label: Error Handling
examplePhrases: 400 for invalid URL, 409 if alias taken
404 when code not found, 410 for expired URLs

optionalRequirements.label: Analytics Endpoint
optionalRequirements.description: GET endpoint to retrieve click stats and analytics
examplePhrases: GET /api/v1/urls/:code/stats returns click count
Analytics endpoint returns clickCount, createdAt, and lastAccessedAt

optionalRequirements.label: Update Endpoint
optionalRequirements.description: PATCH or PUT endpoint to modify URL properties
examplePhrases: PATCH /api/v1/urls/:code to update expiration or destination
PUT requires authentication to modify URL properties

optionalRequirements.label: Delete Endpoint
examplePhrases: DELETE /api/v1/urls/:code requires authentication
Returns 204 on successful deletion

requiredEndpoints.purpose: Create a new short URL from a long URL
requiredEndpoints.exampleNotes: POST with body containing url, optional customAlias, and optional expiresAt. Returns shortId and shortUrl. Errors: 400 (invalid URL), 409 (alias taken).

requiredEndpoints.purpose: Redirect to the original long URL
requiredEndpoints.exampleNotes: GET /{shortId} returns HTTP 301/302 redirect to original URL. Returns 404 if not found or expired.

requiredEndpoints.purpose: Retrieve analytics and metrics for a short URL
requiredEndpoints.exampleNotes: Returns clicks count, lastAccessed timestamp, and referrers list. Requires auth if user-specific.

optionalEndpoints.exampleNotes: DELETE requires authentication. Returns 204 on success, 403 if unauthorized.

optionalEndpoints.purpose: List all URLs for authenticated user
optionalEndpoints.exampleNotes: Returns paginated list of user's URLs. Requires authentication.
PUT to update expiration or destination. Requires auth.

improvementQuestions.question: Your single service handles both writes and reads. Given the 100:1 read-to-write ratio, how could splitting into two services (one for URL creation, one for redirection) allow independent scaling?
With billions of redirects per day and a sub-100ms latency requirement, what layer between your redirect service and database would dramatically reduce load for popular URLs?
To implement cache-aside for your redirect service, how should the service, cache, and database connect? (Hint: Service checks cache first, then database on miss)

feedbackTemplates.missing: How will incoming HTTP requests from users reach your backend? What component typically sits at the edge to route traffic?
feedbackTemplates.present: Good! Your API Gateway will route incoming requests to the appropriate services.

feedbackTemplates.missing: What component will handle the core business logic (URL shortening and redirection)? Think about what sits between your API Gateway and database.
feedbackTemplates.present: Good! Service component is present to handle core business logic.
feedbackTemplates.bonusForTwo: Excellent! Having two services allows you to scale reads and writes independently for the 100:1 read-to-write ratio.

feedbackTemplates.present: Excellent! Cache will dramatically improve redirect latency for popular URLs.

feedbackTemplates.missing: Where will URL mappings be permanently stored so they survive crashes? What provides durable persistence?
feedbackTemplates.present: Good choice! Database will reliably store all URL mappings.

criticalPaths.name: Basic Data Flow Path
criticalPaths.description: The core path from API Gateway through Service to Database for basic functionality

feedbackTemplates.missing: How do requests flow from your API Gateway to the database? Connect the components to form a complete data path.
feedbackTemplates.present: Perfect! Your basic data flow is complete: API Gateway → Service → Database.
feedbackTemplates.suboptimal: Your data path exists but check the flow. The core path should be: API Gateway → Service → Database.

architecturePatterns.name: No Direct Backend Access from Client
architecturePatterns.description: Web clients should not connect directly to backend components like Database or Cache

feedbackTemplates.present: Good! Your clients access backend services through the API Gateway (proper separation of concerns).
feedbackTemplates.incorrect: Should Web clients directly access your Database, Cache, or Service? What layer provides security, rate limiting, and proper routing? Consider removing the direct connection and routing through API Gateway.

architecturePatterns.name: API Gateway Routes to Services
architecturePatterns.description: API Gateway must connect to Service nodes to route requests

feedbackTemplates.missing: After your API Gateway receives requests, what handles the business logic? How should the API Gateway route requests to your application layer?
feedbackTemplates.present: Good! Your API Gateway correctly routes requests to services.
feedbackTemplates.incorrect: Should your API Gateway directly query the Database or Cache? What layer typically handles business logic and data access between the gateway and data stores?

architecturePatterns.name: Services Access Database
architecturePatterns.description: Service nodes must connect to the database to persist and retrieve URL mappings

feedbackTemplates.missing: How will your services persist and retrieve URL mappings? What connection is needed between your application logic and data storage?
feedbackTemplates.present: Great! Your services can access the database for persistence.
feedbackTemplates.incorrect: Check your service to database connections.

architecturePatterns.name: Cache Layer for Performance
architecturePatterns.description: Optional cache layer connected between Service and Database for optimized reads

feedbackTemplates.present: Excellent! Your service can check cache first, then fall back to database on cache miss.

architecturePatterns.name: Split Read and Write Services
architecturePatterns.description: Bonus for introducing a second service so reads and writes can scale independently.

feedbackTemplates.present: Great call! Splitting reads and writes across two services lets you scale the 100:1 traffic pattern independently.

architecturePatterns.name: Complete Request Flow
architecturePatterns.description: There should be a complete path from API Gateway through services to the database

feedbackTemplates.missing: Ensure there's a complete flow: API Gateway → Services → Database. This allows requests to flow through your system.
feedbackTemplates.incorrect: Check that your components are properly connected to allow data flow.

architecturePatterns.name: Cache Should Not Connect Directly to Database
architecturePatterns.description: Services should mediate between Cache and Database, not direct connection

feedbackTemplates.present: Good! Your cache is properly connected through services.
feedbackTemplates.incorrect: In the cache-aside pattern, which component mediates between Cache and Database? Should Cache directly talk to Database, or should Service orchestrate the lookup?

architecturePatterns.name: Database Should Not Connect to Cache
architecturePatterns.description: Database should not initiate connections to Cache

feedbackTemplates.present: Good architecture! Database doesn't initiate connections to cache.
feedbackTemplates.incorrect: Does your Database actively populate the cache, or does a Service handle caching logic? Think about separation of concerns - databases store data, services implement business logic.

architecturePatterns.name: Database Should Not Connect to API Gateway
architecturePatterns.description: Database is a backend component and should not connect to API Gateway

feedbackTemplates.present: Good! Database is properly isolated in the backend.
feedbackTemplates.incorrect: Should your Database push data to clients or to the API Gateway? What's the typical direction of data flow in a web architecture?

architecturePatterns.name: Service Should Not Connect to Web
architecturePatterns.description: Services respond to API Gateway, not directly to Web clients

feedbackTemplates.present: Good! Services don't connect directly to clients.
feedbackTemplates.incorrect: Should your backend Service directly communicate with Web clients? What layer should handle client-facing concerns like authentication, rate limiting, and routing?

architecturePatterns.name: Service Should Not Connect Back to API Gateway
architecturePatterns.description: Services receive requests from API Gateway, they don't connect back to it

feedbackTemplates.present: Good! Request flow goes in the correct direction.
feedbackTemplates.incorrect: Do Services initiate requests to the API Gateway, or do they receive requests from it? Think about the request-response pattern - who calls whom?
